preFix.title: Fitting stair handrail brackets and rail
preFix.subtitle: Fix a timber handrail to a staircase wall on standard brackets
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a timber handrail to a staircase wall using standard wall brackets, covering bracket spacing, setting the correct height and angle, fixing into masonry or stud partitions, and securing the rail to the brackets. Covers both straight runs and angled returns at top and bottom.
preFix.sourceNotes: BS 8300: Design of buildings and their approaches to meet the needs of disabled people. Building Regulations Approved Document K (UK). City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A wall-mounted stair handrail is a simple job once the correct

is established. The critical measurement is taken from the pitch line (the diagonal line across the stair nosings), not from the step surface; marking from the wrong datum puts the rail at the wrong height on every bracket. The rail should feel comfortable to grasp without bending sideways at the top of the stair.

Handrail section in the chosen profile (D-section, full round, or moulded) cut to length. Handrail brackets (typically 3 to 4 per metre, depending on the rail weight). 60 mm No. 10 screws with matching wall plugs for masonry fixing. 75 mm No. 10 screws for stud wall fixing (to reach through plasterboard into the stud). End caps or wall returns if the rail terminates at a wall rather than a newel post.
Step 1, establish the pitch line and bracket height

Hold a long batten or level across the nosings of several stairs to establish the pitch line visually. Mark the pitch line on the wall with a pencil using the long spirit level: set the level at the pitch angle and draw a line parallel to the stair run. Measure 900 mm perpendicular to the pitch line (not vertically from the step surface) and mark the rail height line at each bracket position. The height measured from the tread surface will be slightly different at each step as you go up or down; measuring from the pitch line gives a consistent height along the full run.
Step 2, mark and fix the brackets

Space the brackets at 900 mm centres maximum along the rail run; fix one within 150 mm of each end and one in the middle of any span over 1500 mm. Hold each bracket against the wall at the marked height and mark the fixing holes. Drill into masonry with the SDS drill at the marked positions and insert wall plugs. Screw the brackets finger-tight first, check alignment along the rail height line, then drive all screws fully home.
Step 3, cut and fit the rail

Cut the rail to length with the tenon saw. The cut ends at top and bottom should be square across the rail profile if the rail returns to the wall; angle-cut to align flush with the wall if the rail continues past the last bracket to a decorative scroll or return. Sand the cut ends and any rough surfaces with the sanding block. Lay the rail in the bracket saddles and fix with the bracket top screws or straps provided. Check the rail is secure and cannot rock or lift from any bracket.
Step 4, fit returns and end caps

Fit a curved return or scroll to the wall end of the rail so the end is not a sharp exposed cut edge. These are available as pre-made timber returns for all standard profiles, glued and pinned or screwed from below. Fill any visible screw holes in the rail surface with matching wood filler, sand, and finish to match the surrounding timber.

attrs.fix: Locate studs with a detector and re-fix the brackets with longer screws (100 mm) that reach into the stud. On a masonry wall, check that the wall plug is seated correctly and is the right size for the screw used.
attrs.cause: Brackets are screwed into plasterboard only, not reaching the stud or masonry behind.
attrs.symptom: Rail flexes or creaks when pushed sideways

attrs.fix: The only fix is to re-drill the bracket positions at the correct height. Fill the old holes with filler matching the wall finish before re-drilling.
attrs.cause: The height was measured from the tread surface rather than from the pitch line, or the pitch line angle was set incorrectly.
attrs.symptom: Rail height feels wrong when gripping — too high or too low

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
